video_title: Is Affirm outgrowing credit cards? Max Levchin explains
transcript: All right, new episode of Power Players and I'm sitting down with a firm founder, Max Lechin. I've talked to Max for years. I always love when he comes by Yahoo Finance HQ in New York City. Not only is Max the founder of a firm, this company that is doing more than buy now, pay later, it's doing cards, it's thinking about how to reddo engineering jobs in this age of AI. Max is the co-founder of PayPal. You remember PayPal? You still see PayPal out in the stores. Well, Max helped co-found it. He worked with Elon Musk, Reed Hoffman to create PayPal and really the whole digital payment space. He's the OG of fintech and he's on with me for power players. So, I want to talk about the business of a firm and of course you just came off your investor forum, not investor day, a little bit different. Um, but I never asked you what was the difference between what is the difference between like a being a founder? You founded this company, but your credit is co-founding PayPal. Like what's the difference between those two? >> Uh, so reality is I co-founded this one, too. Uh, I've been a solo founder in the past. Uh, it doesn't work as well as people think it does. It's very challenging to be entirely in your own head all the time in the early days when you sort of think I got it. I have a plan. It's a vision and you don't have a counterpart to be like, hey, am I crazy or is this really a thing? And vice versa like are we really toast? Like are we done? Like you you want to have that couch ideally fully reciprocated by your co-founders where they can sometimes come hang out on yours and complain to you. And I had that with Peter and uh I was very happy to have a handful of great co-founders early on at a firm. The reason the co has gotten dropped at a firm. It's been 15ish years >> actually. I didn't realize it. So yeah, >> it's been a long time and uh one by one my co-founders are like this is great but you seem to really enjoy running it and I got this other thing I want to do. So one by one they went off to do another startup uh join a VC firm other things. So, I think uh at some point our head of comms back in the day said, you know, you're a co-founder, but when people ask where the other ones are, you're like, oh, not not here. >> I feel bad. I actually did not know that after all these years talking to the website as founder. >> I am one of four. Okay. It turns out, but uh yeah, I've I've been the last remaining last standing. No, last remaining. >> Last remaining. You're doing good. We're still friends. We're all still friends. >> How did you know? So in those early days, like how did you know what test you were focused on and the other three folks were going to be focused on? >> So the cool thing, so I I'd love to claim extreme foresight, but I don't didn't know any better. Um, I I've I've been heard recently saying that this is by far the best time in history to run a company as an engineer where just the sheer abundance of engineering amazingness that's happening all around us with AI codegen tools and just all the things that you can do better than most people if you have a good understanding of how AI works, what you can do with it, what it cannot yet do, all all the sort of technical details that we have and have taken for granted as computer scientists, as computer engineers is just it's an unbelievable advantage. Like a company that is not run by an engineer is not as good a stock bet in my opinion today as one run by somebody with a CS degree. And 15 years ago, that was not obviously a thing. But I did start a company with three other CS majors. And so we actually only knew professionally how to do more or less one thing, all four of us. But as a result, we sort of got into this habit of like, hey, you're gonna go do this thing and I'm going to do that other thing. Neither one of us really qualified, but both smart people. We have engineering mindsets. We know how to build stuff. We're just going to get it from first principles. That actually served me really well. The current executive team at a firm. If you look at sort of we've been together for a long time and many people have had their roles fairly suddenly changed like you're doing this, ah, we're going to go do that other thing now. Not necessarily computer engineering. Incidentally, our chief operating officer is a mechanical engineer and a great one.
